summary:
The Paisley Caves are a series of five caves in the Summer Lake Basin of south-central Oregon, USA, that have yielded some of the most compelling evidence for pre-Clovis human occupation in North America. The key evidence consists of human coprolites (fossil dung) recovered from sediment layers dated to approximately 12,300 BCE — older than the Clovis cultural horizon — that contain human mitochondrial DNA confirming their origin from anatomically modern Homo sapiens.

In addition to the pre-Clovis coprolites, the Paisley Caves contain a long sequence of archaeological deposits spanning from the late Pleistocene through more recent periods. The late Pleistocene and early Holocene layers (encompassing the period 10,000–8,000 BCE) document Paleo-Indian occupation of the Great Basin interior, with faunal remains including horse, camel, and pronghorn antelope alongside stone tools. The discovery of pre-Clovis human DNA at Paisley Caves — along with sites such as [[event:1286|Meadowcroft Rockshelter]] and [[event:1285|Monte Verde]] — has contributed significantly to the revision of the Clovis First model of American colonization, supporting earlier human presence in the Americas than previously accepted.
